title: Expressing support for the designation of April 30, 2025, as "National Adult Hepatitis B Vaccination Awareness Day".
plain_summary: This bill asks Congress to make April 30, 2025 a special day to remind adults about getting vaccinated for hepatitis B. Hepatitis B is a serious disease that hurts the liver and affects millions of Americans. Even though the vaccine works really well and stops 95 out of 100 people from getting sick, only 30 out of 100 adults have gotten the shot. The bill wants more people to get tested, get vaccinated, and learn about hepatitis B so fewer people get sick and die from this disease.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses support for making April thirtieth, twenty twenty-five a national day to raise awareness about hepatitis B vaccinations for adults.

sections.title: Facts About Hepatitis B
sections.text: Up to two point four million people in the United States have a long-term hepatitis B infection, and up to two-thirds of these people do not know they are infected. Hepatitis B is a virus that attacks the liver and spreads through infected blood and other body fluids. It can pass from mothers to their babies during birth and through sharing needles for drug use. This disease affects communities of color including Asian Americans, Pacific Islanders, and African immigrants more than others. It also affects sexual and gender minorities and people struggling with opioid addiction. People with other diseases like diabetes, HIV, hepatitis C, and liver disease are at higher risk of getting hepatitis B too. People on kidney dialysis (a treatment that cleans the blood when kidneys fail) also face higher risk. There is no cure for hepatitis B, so people with the long-term infection need medical care for their entire lives. Having hepatitis B for a long time is one of the most common causes of liver cancer. One out of every four people with untreated long-term hepatitis B will develop liver cancer, scarring of the liver, or liver failure. Liver cancer has only an eighteen percent survival rate after five years in the United States. Safe and very effective vaccines exist to protect against hepatitis B. Since the nineteen nineties, babies and children in the United States have been routinely vaccinated against hepatitis B. All adults aged nineteen to fifty-nine should get the hepatitis B vaccine. Adults sixty and older who have risk factors for hepatitis B should also get vaccinated. The hepatitis B vaccine is ninety-five percent effective and was the first vaccine developed to prevent cancer. From nineteen ninety to twenty twenty, this vaccine is expected to have prevented three hundred ten million cases of hepatitis B around the world. However, only thirty percent of adults in the United States are vaccinated against hepatitis B. The number of new acute hepatitis B cases increased by eleven percent nationwide between twenty fourteen and twenty eighteen. Because of the drug use crisis, some regions have seen huge increases in new hepatitis B cases. Maine saw a four hundred eighty-nine percent increase from twenty fifteen to twenty sixteen. Kentucky, West Virginia, and Tennessee saw a one hundred fourteen percent increase from twenty nine to twenty thirteen. Southeastern Massachusetts saw a seventy-eight percent increase in twenty seventeen. North Carolina saw a fifty-six percent increase from twenty fourteen to twenty sixteen. Thirty-six percent of new hepatitis B cases happen among people who inject drugs. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, hepatitis B spreads fifty to one hundred times more easily than HIV and five to ten times more easily than hepatitis C. There are important opportunities to prevent new hepatitis B infections and reduce liver cancer and liver scarring, especially during the current drug use crisis. This can be done by increasing adult hepatitis B vaccination and keeping childhood hepatitis B vaccination rates high.

sections.title: What the House of Representatives Resolves
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports making a National Adult Hepatitis B Vaccination Awareness Day. The House recognizes how important it is to provide support and encouragement for all adults eighteen and older to be tested for hepatitis B at least once in their lives as recommended by health experts. People who could get infected should be vaccinated against hepatitis B. People who are diagnosed with hepatitis B should be connected to proper medical care. To reduce the number of new hepatitis B infections and deaths from hepatitis B, the House encourages a commitment to increasing adult hepatitis B vaccination rates, keeping childhood hepatitis B vaccination rates high, and promoting awareness among healthcare providers and communities about adult hepatitis B vaccination.
